Subscribe here to get it delivered to your inbox, and listen to the Up First podcast for all the news you need to start your day. Today's top stories President Trump is set to travel to Dover Air Force Base today for the dignified transfer of U.S. service members killed in Iranian strikes. The Pentagon yesterday identified the third service member who was killed in Jordan as Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York. The Defense Department earlier this week identified two other service members killed last week as 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas. The number of U.S. casualties killed during the Iran war is now 18. As the number of casualties rises, the president's visit highlights mounting questions about when the war in Iran will end and what its aims are. toggle caption Atta Kenare/AFP via Getty Images 🎧 The public disapproves of U.S. citizens dying in what they potentially see as an unnecessary war, NPR's Franco Ordoñez tells Up First. While the issue of reduced munitions may feel abstract to many, flag-draped coffins are not, Justin Logan from the Cato Institute tells Ordoñez. The death of the 19-year-old Gonzales in particular resonates deeply with people as it underscores the loss of young lives at the hands of this conflict, Logan adds. Politically, Trump is attempting to sell Americans on the idea that things are going well, but the war's casualties tell a different story, Ordoñez says. Sponsor Message Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth yesterday requested $67 billion in supplemental funding for the Pentagon in an hours-long, at times contentious hearing with senators. He says this money would help replenish depleted equipment and munitions and accelerate project and equipment deliveries. Without the additional funding, service members' pay could face shortfalls, Hegseth warns. As fighting intensifies, some lawmakers expressed frustration over being asked to approve funds for a war that Congress never authorized. 🎧 Senators from both parties had a lot of questions for Hegseth on key issues, including Iran's nuclear program and missile capabilities and the future of the Strait of Hormuz, NPR's Claudia Grisales says. Their skepticism highlights concerns about the war's direction, especially as midterm elections are right around the corner. The war has created challenges for Republicans, but has provided Democrats with opportunities, Grisales says. Democratic Sen. Patty Murray says rising gasoline prices are linked to the recent escalation in fighting, a message Democrats will likely use to try to win back control of Congress. Arizona Republicans chose U.S. Rep. Andy Biggs as their nominee for governor and state lawmaker Alexander Kolodin as the secretary of state nominee in yesterday's primaries. Both candidates have openly opposed the 2020 presidential election results. Trump endorsed both candidates, and Turning Point, the political group founded in Arizona by the late Charlie Kirk, backed them. Sponsor Message 🎧 Trump and Turning Point's support hold less weight in the general election , where MAGA candidates must appeal to a broader electorate, especially Arizona's crucial independent voters, says Ben Giles of NPR member station KJZZ. The primary results have some Arizona Republicans worried that Democrats could reclaim key races in the state. Although there were some more traditional conservative candidates in the primary elections for both races, neither race was close, underscoring Trump and Turning Point's significant influence among primary voters, Giles says. Republican primary voters prefer far-right candidates to represent their interests, even though those candidates continue to lose when Democrats and independents have a say in November. So far, Turning Point has struggled to get its preferred candidates elected in important statewide races, according to Giles. ➡️ Trump has campaigned more so far this year than he did at this point of the year in 2018, during his first term. While low approval ratings usually keep a president away from competitive midterm races, Trump and his team are taking a different approach. The House of Representatives yesterday voted to extend government funding past this year's midterms, through Dec. 4. Democratic leaders opposed the extension, saying it "fails to block funding for Donald Trump's violent mass deportation machine," though six Democrats joined with Republicans to advance the bill. It now moves to the Senate, where it needs Democratic support to pass. If it doesn't, the government risks running out of money in September, right before the midterms. Today's listen toggle caption Arif Ali/AFP via Getty Images The orange metro line in Lahore, Pakistan's second-largest city, has revolutionized travel and safety for women since its opening six years ago. Women in Lahore have long depended on public transport like buses and rickshaws to get around the congested city, but they have often faced safety concerns and harassment. The metro has provided women with their own separate section as an additional safety barrier. NPR's Diaa Hadid rode the metro to hear directly from women about how the orange line train has improved their lives. Sponsor Message Life advice toggle caption Jesse Finkelstein/Sonya Barlas/NPR There are many ways to cope with life's stressors, and not all of them are healthy. Shireen Rizvi and Jesse Finkelstein, co-authors of Real Skills for Real Life: A DBT Guide to Navigating Stress, Emotions, and Relationships, use engaging diagrams and illustrations to teach effective ways to accept distress and respond to it effectively. As a board-certified dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) therapist, Rizvi shares a set of techniques from her book called TIP, which stands for temperature, intense exercise, paced breathing and paired muscle relaxation. She says these four skills can help in moments when you're so distressed you can't think straight 🌡️ Temperature: Plunge your face, eyes and temples into a bowl of cold water and ice for as long as possible. This triggers the mammalian dive reflex, which helps mammals increase their chances of survival by slowing their heart and respiratory rates. Plunge your face, eyes and temples into a bowl of cold water and ice for as long as possible. This triggers the mammalian dive reflex, which helps mammals increase their chances of survival by slowing their heart and respiratory rates. 🏃‍♀️ Intense exercise: Do something to get your heart rate up quickly, such as running up stairs. You should sustain that movement for 30 seconds or longer. When you stop, and your heart rate decreases, you'll feel calmer. Do something to get your heart rate up quickly, such as running up stairs. You should sustain that movement for 30 seconds or longer. When you stop, and your heart rate decreases, you'll feel calmer. 🏃‍♀️ Paced breathing: Make your exhales longer than your inhales. This will help avoid hyperventilating, slow your heart rate and reduce emotional intensity. Make your exhales longer than your inhales. This will help avoid hyperventilating, slow your heart rate and reduce emotional intensity. 💪 Paired muscle relaxation: Tense your muscles as tight as you can and then release them, one muscle group at a time. Breathe in as you clench and breathe out as you relax. Pair your exhales with a calming word. You get a greater sense of relaxation this way than if you try to relax without tensing the muscle first, Rizvi says. topic: Police Madison Wisconsin Sparking Protests Officer

left.title: Police officer fatally shoots man in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

left-center.title: Man shot and killed by police in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

farLeftText: A police officer in Madison, Wisconsin, shot and killed a man Wednesday in an incident that was captured on cellphone footage, sparking local protests. John Patterson, the Madison police chief, acknowledged the shooting at a news conference, but stressed footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. “I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss,” Patterson said. The dead man was in his 30s, Patterson said, but police did not release his name. The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than 2 miles from the state capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded. “Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name,” said Brandi Grayson, the head of Urban Triage, a Madison-based Black community advocacy group. “That is the cruelty of state violence.” A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday with people chanting: “Arrest the cops!” Police had been responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, Patterson said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. In one of the videos circulating online, three officers are seen holding the man down and attempting to arrest him. The officers lose control of the suspect, who frees his arms and attempts to get up off his knees. One officer appears to reach for his pistol, when a female officer runs toward the others shouting: “Taser! Taser!” That officer did not draw his firearm. After some jostling, the suspect falls to the ground. As he goes down, another officer rises to his feet, pulls out his pistol, leans down toward the man on the ground and fires three shots at close range. The officer who fired the shots then re-holsters his gun and places the suspect’s arm behind his back, before standing up and walking away. Two other officers push the suspect over onto his belly, before others arrive to attempt to offer medical attention. The officer who fired the lethal shots had been injured by the suspect with a knife, Patterson said. He described the officer as a veteran of the police force, but didn’t provide his name. Another officer was injured, but it’s not clear how, Patterson said. The other two officers were uninjured. The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. Sabrina Madison, the city council president, called the videos “both disturbing and painful”, in a statement. “I’ve received several calls from alders with questions and what I can say without a doubt is that I expect a full, comprehensive, and transparent independent investigation into today’s shooting of a community member,” the statement reads. The Wisconsin department of justice criminal investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers. A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer had been justified. The Associated Press contributed
centerLeftText: A Madison police officer shot and killed a man who had injured him with a knife while resisting arrest, the city's police chief said Wednesday. Police have not identified the man who was killed, saying only that he was in his 30s. "I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss," Madison Police Chief John Patterson said at a news conference Wednesday. Cellphone video of the shooting circulated on social media shortly after the incident. Three gunshots can be heard on the cellphone video. Patterson stressed that the footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. "I ask the community to recognize that there is other video that they have not seen, that's not circulating online, that will be reviewed as part of this investigation," he said. "So, to the extent that they can as we all navigate this trauma together, we try to remain calm and remember that not all perspectives are online right now." Madison Police Chief John Patterson speaking at a news conference after a Madison police officer shot and killed a man in Madison, Wis., Wednesday, July 22, 2026. WKOW via AP The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than two miles from the state Capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded. A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer was justified. His family accepted a $3.35 million settlement from the city in 2017. The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. "Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name," said Brandi Grayson, the head of the Madison-based Black community advocacy group Urban Triage. "That is the cruelty of state violence." Gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong speaks alongside protesters near where a Madison police officer shot and killed a man Wednesday, July 22, 2026, in Madison, Wis. AP Photo/Kayla Wolf A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday, with some people chanting, "Arrest the cops!" Among the protesters who gathered throughout the night was Wisconsin gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong. "We're gonna make sure that we're able to support any assembly that might happen, any protest that might happen, but also not wanting to amplify any issues or create friction where it's unnecessary, frankly, so we'll be reaching out to community members and community leaders to hopefully strike the right balance," Patterson said. Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers said in a social media post he was "briefed on the officer-involved shooting that occurred in Madison today" and continues "to receive updates as more information is released." "There must be transparency and accountability in any investigation moving forward," Evers said in the post. Police were responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, the police chief said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers, and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. An officer deployed a taser but it was unsuccessful, the chief said. The same officer who was injured by the knife fired his weapon and the man who was shot later died at the hospital, the police chief said. He described the officer who fired his weapon as a veteran officer, but did not reveal any other details. "When a knife is produced during officers' apprehension arrest, the overall situation is clearly elevated. It becomes incredibly dangerous and it obviously increases risks for everyone there," Patterson said. "For every officer. For the person as well. I think it severely narrows the scope of options and possible outcomes." "This was not a small, folding knife. It's a large, fixed-blade knife," Patterson added, telling reporters a photo of the knife would eventually be shared. "My understanding is there are some witnesses that also saw this, so I think we gotta, as a community, recognize we don't have all the information right now, and we have to allow our investigators to piece things together and get as clear a picture as we can," he said. Another officer was injured, but it's not clear how, Patterson said, noting police don't believe that officer's injuries were caused by the knife. The other two officers were uninjured. All four officers will be placed on administrative leave, as is standard protocol for the department, during the investigation. The Wisconsin Department of Criminal Investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers.

topic: Tropical Storm Bertha Louisiana Moves Heads

left-center.title: Tropical Storm Bertha moves off Louisiana, heads for Texas. See its path.

center.title: News Wrap: Tropical Storm Bertha makes landfall in Louisiana

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
Tropical Storm Bertha was turning toward Texas early Thursday after making landfall over Louisiana on Wednesday then heading offshore again. It continued to bring rain to a large swath of the northern Gulf Coast along its path.

Bertha was moving westward and "is expected to make landfall along the northwestern Gulf Coast today," the National Hurricane Center said. The storm was forecast to maintain tropical storm strength before dissipating inland over Texas Thursday night or Friday.

Map shows projected path

Bertha, the second named storm of the Atlantic hurricane season, has had maximum sustained winds of 45 mph after a gradual decrease since Tuesday night.

CBS News

"On the forecast track, the center of Bertha will move near or along the Louisiana and upper Texas coasts through this afternoon, and then move farther inland into Texas this evening and tonight," the center said.

Louisiana state climatologist Jay Grymes said Wednesday he expected some pockets of flooding, but added, "With luck, this will be an in-and-out kind of situation." It appears to have been so.

Warnings in effect

A tropical storm warning is posted for the Gulf Coast from the Terrebonne/Lafourche Parish line in Louisiana to Sargent, Texas.

"Tropical storm conditions will continue spreading across portions of the northern and northwestern Gulf Coast within the warning area through today," the center said.

"Bertha is expected to produce total rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches with isolated totals to 6 inches through tonight along the Texas Gulf Coast and South Texas. This rainfall may produce isolated flash flooding, especially in urban areas," the center cautioned.

The system could also produce storm surge, raising waters up to 3 feet in some locations along the coast.

Bertha comes weeks after the 2026 hurricane season's first named storm, Tropical Storm Arthur, which made landfall in June along the Gulf Coast of Texas.
centerText: In our news wrap Wednesday, Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall on the coast of Louisiana about 40 miles east of New Orleans, this year's cyclosporiasis outbreak in the U.S. is officially the worst on record and OpenAI says it's investigating an "unprecedented cyber incident" after one of its AI models went rogue and hacked another company. Notice: Transcripts are machine and human generated and lightly edited for accuracy. They may contain errors. Amna Nawaz: In the day's other news: Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall on the southern coast of Louisiana this afternoon about 40 miles east of New Orleans. Satellite imagery shows the sheer size of the storm, with winds stretching as far as 100 miles from its center. Officials are warning of dangerous storm surge and damaging wind, even as Bertha weakens moving westward across the Gulf Coast. In a press conference today, Louisiana's governor said the state is prepared for the worst, though the storm is not expected to cause major damage. Gov. Jeff Landry (R-LA): Today's forecast is encouraging, but it doesn't mean that we should be complacent. I expect every level of government to stay focused on, and that's its core responsibility of protecting people and maintaining the critical infrastructure. Amna Nawaz: Meantime, flash flooding has swamped homes and businesses in West Virginia, with up to seven inches of rain falling in some areas. Governor Patrick Morrisey declared a state of emergency for all counties and sent more than 300 National Guard troops to affected areas. Emergency crews on the other side of the Atlantic are battling conditions of a different extreme. Wildfire has engulfed entire homes in Turkey, while, in the mountains of Sicily, towns were surrounded by a wall of smoke. In Spain, a fire in Guadalajara, north of Madrid, has burned some 80,000 acres and forced more than 1,200 people to evacuate. It's now the country's second largest wildfire on record. Today, Spain's prime minister visited the fire zone. He said, compared to this time last year, the number of wildfires in Spain has quadrupled. Pedro Sanchez, Spanish Prime Minister (through translator): Spain needs a major state pact against the climate emergency, because we cannot only think about wildfires when they occur. We have to act beforehand. This is not about politics. It is not about ideology. It is science. Amna Nawaz: The fires come in the middle of a still sweltering summer across Europe. In France, public health officials say there were upwards of 5,700 more deaths than usual during a record-setting heat wave last month. This year's cyclosporiasis outbreak in the U.S. is officially the worst on record, as the CDC says there are more than 4,100 confirmed cases across 41 states. The agency is aware of 7,400 additional cases that have not been confirmed. No deaths have been reported. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. told ABC News yesterday that the outbreak is -- quote -- "under control." Officials are still investigating iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms as a potential source of the outbreak and say the number of cases could rise further. On Capitol Hill today, the House passed a flurry of Republican-led measures, including the GOP's $95 billion budget proposal that would further fund the Iran war. Woman: The concurrent resolution is adopted. Amna Nawaz: The tally was 216 to 214, with all Democrats opposed. The vast majority of the funds would go towards the Pentagon and other national security needs. The rest would help farmers struggling under President Trump's tariffs and pay for voting law changes that he's pushing. The measure faces an uphill battle in the Senate, but House Speaker Mike Johnson touted it and other bills passed today as a win. Rep. Mike Johnson (R-LA): This was obviously a consequential week for House Republicans, but it was a very consequential week for the American people. Amna Nawaz: The House also approved measures that would ban insider trading for lawmakers and officially renamed the Defense Department as the Department of War. OpenAI says it's investigating what is called an unprecedented cyber incident after one of its A.I. models went rogue and hacked another company. The ChatGPT maker says the breach happened last week while OpenAI was testing the cybersecurity capabilities of its systems. The head of the hacked company, Hugging Face, called the attack mind-blowing, but said there was -- quote -- "no malicious intent." The incident has raised concerns about the growing capabilities of A.I. and the need for guardrails to keep the technology in check. On Wall Street today, stocks ended a bit lower as oil prices spiked. The Dow Jones industrial average lost just six points, so basically flat. The Nasdaq fell nearly 150 points. The S&P 500 also closed the day in negative territory. And we have two passings to acknowledge this evening, starting with the death of former CIA Director James Woolsey. His tenure under President Bill Clinton was a rocky one, marked by the 1994 arrest of CIA case officer Aldrich Ames, who sold secrets to the Soviet Union for nearly a decade. Woolsey's decision to not fire or demote anyone in the wake of the scandal cast a shadow over his time at the CIA. His politics tilted to the right over the years. In 2016, he joined Donald Trump's presidential campaign as a senior adviser. And he continued to defend America's 2003 invasion of Iraq, which he spoke about on this program in 2016. James Woolsey, Former CIA Director: Two of the three weapons of mass destruction, biological and chemical, were in the hands of Saddam the whole time. It was the third kind that everybody's particularly worried about, nuclear, that was lied about and so forth. But two-thirds of the types of weapons of mass destruction were in fact in Iraq. Amna Nawaz: His wife says James Woolsey died at his home in Washington from a stroke. He was 84 years old. And celebrated saxophonist Plas Johnson has died. He's best known for his playful musical turn on one of the most recognizable movie themes in history. Johnson's recording for composer Henry Mancini's "The Pink Panther" helped the song to win three Grammys and an Oscar nomination. The Louisiana native was also a prolific session musician, recording alongside the likes of Frank Sinatra, Ella Fitzgerald, and the Beach Boys, among many others. Johnson's family says he died last week at his home in Los Angeles. Plas Johnson was 94 years old.

topic: House Adopts Billion Budget Iran Gops

left-center.title: House Adopts $95 Billion G.O.P. Budget for Iran War

right.title: House Adopts GOP's $95 Billion Budget Plan for Iran War, Trump Priorities

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
House Republicans on Wednesday won narrow approval of their $95 billion budget plan to fund the war with Iran and impose new election restrictions President Trump has demanded, muscling through dissent in their ranks to send the measure to a skeptical Senate.

The blueprint would pave the way for filibuster-proof legislation known as a reconciliation bill that would bypass Democratic opposition and provide up to $73 billion in additional military funding, $12 billion for farmers and $10 billion to enforce severe new voting restrictions championed by Mr. Trump.

It was approved in a 216-to-214 vote, with all but two Republicans in favor despite considerable concern in their ranks about its cost, its scope and voting to fund an unpopular war just months before the midterm elections.

The plan faces an uphill fight in the Senate, where the Republican leader has cast doubt on his chamber’s willingness to embrace it.

topic: Louvre Gallery Reopens Heist Brazen Jewel

left-center.title: Louvre Gallery Reopens After Brazen Jewel Heist

center.title: Watch: Louvre reopens gallery after jewellery heist

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
The Louvre Museum in Paris reopened its Galerie d'Apollon (Gallery of Apollo) to visitors on Wednesday, nine months after jewellery worth millions was stolen in a brazen heist.

But the gallery, which once housed France's crown jewels, reopened without any on display.

"This is an iconic space within the Louvre, but it will no longer house the Crown Jewels and gemstones, which are to be displayed in secure areas elsewhere in the museum", said the museum's director, Christophe Leribault.

The heist took place on 19 October when masked thieves used a truck with a set of mechanical ladders mounted on it to gain access to the gallery via a balcony.

Police have arrested four people who prosecutors allege are the thieves - but the stolen items have still not been recovered.

centerText: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn. It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889. The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage. Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old. Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany. The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries. Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it. "This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said. A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station. For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism.

topic: Trump Destroy Bridge Power Plant Strait

center.title: Trump says U.S. will destroy a bridge or power plant for each Iranian attack in the Strait of Hormuz

right.title: Trump Warns U.S Will 'Bomb And Destroy' Bridge or Power Plant if Iran Shoots Ships in Strait of Hormuz

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: The U.S. carried out another wave of strikes on Iran overnight into Wednesday, and air defenses opened fire over the capital, Tehran. Iran launched a missile attack on a Jordanian city on Israel's doorstep, and alerts were issued in Bahrain and Saudi Arabia. Trump's latest threat to target civilian infrastructure came as the unpopular war spirals with no clear off-ramp, threatening further worldwide economic disruption and sending fuel prices up ahead of midterm U.S. elections this fall. Both sides have dug in on their dispute over the strait, a crucial waterway for global energy that remains largely closed because of Iranian attacks. DUBAI, United Arab Emirates (AP) — President Donald Trump warned Wednesday that the U.S. will destroy one bridge or power plant each time Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz. With negotiations largely stalled, both sides have sought leverage by targeting civilian infrastructure. Iran has responded to U.S. attacks by targeting energy infrastructure and desalination plants that provide drinking water in parched neighboring Gulf countries. WATCH: Trump attends dignified transfer of 4 U.S. service members killed in the Middle East International law generally prohibits such attacks unless the infrastructure is being used for military purposes. United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres decried such attacks as "unacceptable" on Tuesday. The costs of war are mounting The closure of the strait, through which a fifth of the world's traded oil and gas passed during peacetime, has jolted the world economy with effects far beyond the Middle East. A new threat by Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen to target Saudi shipping in the Red Sea puts another trade chokepoint at risk. "From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT," Trump wrote on social media. Trump traveled to Dover Air Force Base on Wednesday for the arrival of the remains of four U.S. service members. A day earlier, his defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, estimated in a fiery Senate hearing that the U.S. had spent $37.5 billion on the war so far. WATCH: Hegseth says Iran war has cost $37.5B so far and Pentagon needs $67B to restock arms Benchmark Brent crude oil rose to more than $93 a barrel in trading. That's up from less than $72 early this month, which is roughly where it was before the war. Gasoline prices are also climbing. Iran says it has the right to manage traffic and potentially charge fees in the strait, which was open to all and toll-free before the war. It has attacked ships using a route through the strait that is overseen by U.S. forces and intended to be outside Tehran's control. Iran's parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, who has led negotiations with the U.S., appeared to brush off Trump's threats. "If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe, and the security of the strait is in the absence of American forces," he wrote on social media. "We have repeatedly said that the situation of the strait will not return to prewar conditions." Attacks shake Iran and Jordan Jordan's military said it intercepted four Iranian missiles over Aqaba, while two others fell in "uninhabited areas." Plumes of smoke could be seen overhead in videos filmed by people in the nearby Israeli city of Eilat. Jordan said it also intercepted four Iranian drones. Iran has avoided striking Israel since hostilities resumed last month, apparently hoping to keep it from reentering the war. But the attack on Aqaba, within sight of Israel's Eilat, raised concerns over a further widening of the conflict after an agreement to halt hostilities collapsed. Another missile alert sounded in Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia warned residents of the Persian Gulf city of Dammam to seek shelter. Saudi authorities later said the danger had passed, without elaborating. The U.S. military said earlier Wednesday that it had completed an 11th night of strikes on Iran, with targets including aircraft hangars and drone storage sites. Iran's state-run IRNA news agency reported explosions across several provinces. Iran's Health Ministry says U.S. strikes over the past 11 days have killed 53 people, including six women and three children, and wounded nearly 600 people. Iran's paramilitary Revolutionary Guard insisted its retaliatory attacks would continue. Diplomatic efforts show no signs of progress Before the latest strikes, Iranian Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni visited Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict, seeking to revive diplomacy. But it was unclear what new arrangement might be reached to end the war, which lately has become a battle for control over the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Interior Ministry spokesman Ali Zeinivand said "diplomacy isn't called off" and that messages were still being exchanged, without elaborating, according to IRNA. An Arab diplomat said Gulf states are increasingly pessimistic about finding an off-ramp for escalating hostilities and are particularly concerned about the latest round of threats in which the two sides have warned they may attack civilian infrastructure. The diplomat said countries in the region, in addition to Pakistan and Turkey, are continuing to push for de-escalation. But, he said the chances for any improvement currently appear bleak. The diplomat spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the situation. At a regional summit in the Philippines, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio denounced Iran's actions in the strait. "If we create a precedent in the Middle East where a nation state can decide that they are going to control an international waterway, charge a toll and if you don't pay them, blow up your ships, we have created a very dangerous precedent which will repeat itself in other parts of the world, including in this region," he said. Rubio said the U.S. remains open to negotiations with Iran, "but right now they don't seem to be serious about that" so instead the focus is on protecting shipping. In response to Iran's attacks, the U.S. re-imposed a blockade on Iranian ports. Trump signals U.S. may attack underground nuclear site Speaking from the Oval Office on Tuesday, Trump gave a dim view on possible talks, saying the U.S. had "no interest in meeting." He signaled that U.S. forces could soon target an under-construction Iranian nuclear site buried deep under a mountain. The site, known as Pickaxe Mountain, lies on the south side of Iran's Natanz nuclear enrichment site, which was bombed by the U.S. during the 12-day war between Iran and Israel in 2025. But Pickaxe Mountain has remained untouched as Iran continues to dig at the site. Iran has always insisted its nuclear program is peaceful, while resuming the enrichment of uranium to near weapons-grade levels since Trump scrapped a 2015 nuclear deal during his first term. The U.S. and others believe Iran had a nuclear weapons program until 2003 and may reconstitute it in the future. Binkley reported from Washington. Contributing were Associated Press writers Bassem Mroue in Beirut; Michelle L. Price and Jim Gomez in Manila, Philippines; and Christopher Weber in Los Angeles.

farRightText: President Donald Trump warned the United States would “bomb and destroy” either a bridge or power plant if Iran “shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.” “From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran,” Trump said in a post on Truth Social. Trump’s post comes as the U.S. “launched strikes on Iran for the 11th consecutive night” on Tuesday, BBC News reported. Trump has also previously “said the next military target could be a complex known as Pickaxe Mountain,” according to the outlet: Earlier the US president said the next military target could be a complex known as Pickaxe Mountain, an underground nuclear site where Iran is suspected of building an undeclared enrichment facility. “We’ll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily,” Trump said, addressing reporters during a White House meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun. In a post on X, U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) explained that Iran’s “military operations centers, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangers, drone storage facilities, and military logistics infrastructure” had been targeted to “degrade Iran’s ability to threaten commercial shipping” in the Strait of Hormuz. “Over the past three months, Iran has attacked more than 30 commercial vessels transiting the international waterway vital for regional and global trade,” CENTCOM continued. “The unwarranted attacks have endangered hundreds of innocent mariners and undermined freedom of navigation.”

Javiel Pena Sola, a Salvadoran citizen, allegedly struck and killed three people traveling in a vehicle on July 18 in Salisbury before he fled the scene. He was found with the help of a police K-9, ICE said. Two victims were pronounced dead at the scene, while a third was airlifted to a hospital where they later died. MAN CHARGED IN CRASH THAT KILLED MOTHER AND TWO YOUNG SONS HAS ICE DETAINER, PRIOR DWI CONVICTIONS "I heard people screaming ... and they pulled the man out; and as they pulled him out, he started screaming," witness Quindel Woods told WSOC-TV. Sola's vehicle was later found in a creek. He is charged with three counts of hit-and-run involving serious injury or death. ICE has lodged a detainer with local authorities to keep him in custody. EXCLUSIVE: DHS SEEKS CUSTODY OF ILLEGAL IMMIGRANT CHARGED WITH MURDER AFTER TRUMP DEPORTATION, BIDEN RELEASE Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis said Sola was arrested in 2016 but was released. She said he entered the United States through Arizona in April 2022. "Although ICE had arrested him in 2016, the Obama Administration RELEASED him into American communities," she said. "These deaths were 100% preventable. Open borders and sanctuary policies have deadly consequences." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Sola's criminal history includes prior convictions for reckless endangerment, failure to stop after an accident involving damage to an attended vehicle, and driving without a license in Maryland in July 2015, as well as an arrest for escaping from custody. After his arrest in Maryland on charges of escaping from custody, law enforcement officials in Baltimore honored an ICE detainer and turned him over to federal agents in March 2016. farRightText: An illegal alien, released from federal custody by the Obama administration, is now accused of killing three Americans in a fatal hit-and-run in Salisbury, North Carolina. Open borders and sanctuary policies have deadly consequences. [Emphasis added] Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officials said Sola crossed the United States-Mexico border in 2002, making his way into Arizona. His criminal record includes convictions for reckless endangerment, failure to stop after an accident involving damage to an attended vehicle, and driving without a license in the sanctuary state of Maryland. Sola was also arrested in Maryland for escaping from custody. Following this arrest, ICE agents lodged a detainer against him. He was turned over to ICE in March 2016, but then the Obama administration released him from federal custody two months later. Sola remains in custody at the Rowan County Jail. ICE agents have lodged a detainer against him, seeking custody if he is released from jail at any time. John Binder is a reporter for Breitbart News. Email him at jbinder@breitbart.com.

topic: Urges Charges Jack Smith Republican Lawmaker

left-center.title: Republican Lawmaker Urges Charges Against Jack Smith

right-center.title: Jim Jordan urges criminal charges against Jack Smith over alleged perjury

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerRightText:
House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan (R-OH) on Wednesday issued a criminal referral of former special counsel Jack Smith to the Department of Justice over allegations he lied to Congress about tracking personal phone messages of dozens of lawmakers.

Republicans have long raised concerns about Smith’s time at President Joe Biden’s DOJ. Smith obtained subpoenas that allowed him to access communications records of mostly Republican lawmakers as part of the DOJ’s and FBI’s “Arctic Frost” investigation. Last week, Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-IA) released data he said indicated Smith’s team reviewed text messages from 44 senators and representatives from both parties, though mostly Republican.

During testimony before the House Judiciary Committee last December, Smith denied that he had examined the contents of lawmakers’ text messages. Jordan said in a letter this week to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that he is submitting the criminal referral due to what he described as new evidence that Smith committed perjury. A DOJ official confirmed to the Washington Examiner that the department “will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct” in the referral.

Advertisement

“All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different,” Jordan wrote in the letter, reported by Fox News. “Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee’s oversight.” “While Mr. Smith testified that he did not seek a search warrant from telecommunications carriers for the content of Members’ text messages, he failed to disclose that his office received text message content from [the National Archives and Records Administration] relating to White House personnel phone records,” Jordan wrote, accusing Smith of having “excluded information that was highly relevant to the Committee’s examination.” During his House testimony last year, Smith claimed his team only sought telephone toll records. Advertisement “Toll records were sought for historical telephone routing information, collected after calls had taken place, identifying the incoming and outgoing call numbers, the time of the calls, and their duration,” he said. “Toll records do not include the content of calls.” Smith is known for leading the Jan. 6, 2021, election-interference and classified-documents cases against President Donald Trump. The Arctic Frost investigation was triggered by Smith’s prosecution of Trump due to alleged election interference in 2020 in the Jan. 6 case. Cruz has said Smith sent subpoenas to AT&T for his cellphone records, voicemails, text messages, and geolocation data. BILL PULTE IS URGED TO REVOKE SECURITY CLEARANCES FOR EX-JACK SMITH TEAM MEMBERS Advertisement Before saying last week that records prove Smith’s team reviewed personal text messages of 44 lawmakers, Grassley had said Smith issued 197 subpoenas to 34 individuals and 163 businesses, including financial institutions, as part of Arctic Frost, in conjunction with former FBI Director Christopher Wray. The subpoenas requested records and communications related to over 430 individuals and organizations, ”all of them appear to be aimed at Republicans,” according to Grassley. Cruz said last November that, in the investigation, U.S. District Judge James Boasberg signed off on subpoenas from Smith’s office that held “no basis,” ordering phone companies to allow the government to monitor the phone lines of nine Republican senators, including himself. Verizon complied with the subpoenas, while AT&T pushed back. “When you have the Biden Department of Justice subpoenaing 20% of the Republicans in the Senate, it is an egregious abuse of process,” Cruz said. “It is a fishing expedition, and we need to have a full and thorough investigation and accountability for every executive branch official who signed off on weaponizing the government.”

topic: Google Billion Play Store Search Fined

left-center.title: Google fined $1 billion by EU over its Play app store and search engine

right-center.title: Google hit with $1 billion EU fine over its Play app store and search

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: Brussels — The European Union on Thursday hit Google with a fine of 890 million euros ($1 billion) after it said the technology behemoth broke digital antitrust regulations by setting up Google Play and its ubiquitous search engine to corral consumers toward its own services and apps, to the detriment of competitors. It was the latest major crackdown on Big Tech by Brussels, which has led the world in reining in some of the world's largest companies from Silicon Valley to Beijing. Google had recently lost its appeal of a $4.5 billion antitrust fine imposed for throttling competition and reducing consumer choice through the dominance of its mobile Android operating system. The European Commission, the bloc's executive branch, said it was acting in the interest of consumers. "The best products should succeed because they're better, not because they're owned by the company running the search engine. And European consumers have a right to be told by app developers where to sign up to the best offers, even when the app store owner does not get a cut," said Teresa Ribera, the commission's Executive Vice President for Clean, Just and Competitive Transition. Google President of Global Affairs Kent Walker blasted the fine as "product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants" that will negatively impact European businesses and consumers. He said that the EU's Digital Markets Act forces Google "to strip away real-time search features Europeans love - like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants - and dismantle safety protections on Google Play." He also asserted that "this isn't fair competition; it's product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants, with European businesses and consumers taking the hit. Regulation should improve products, not make them worse," according to the Reuters news service. "In the EU, businesses have the right to compete fairly. Gatekeepers have the obligation to ensure a level playing field and consumers the right to choose for cheaper alternative offers," European Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier said.

topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Enforce Warrant Mayor

left.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant

left-center.title: Mamdani: NYC Doesn’t Have ‘Legal Authority’ To Enforce ICC Arrest Warrant Against Netanyahu

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall. “I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday. Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a video statement posted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September. Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he had pledged to do during his mayoral campaign last year. On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’s war crimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”. Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him. His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest. “I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations, told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchly pro-Israel Democrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”. “America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.” Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previously called on the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law. The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. “I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court. Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it. Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on the Trump administration to do so. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Donald Trump has already said he will do no such thing. Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the president said in a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in a statement last week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”. Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission. “My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,” said Adil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Tuesday acknowledged the limitations of New York City’s legal authority when it comes to enforcing an arrest warrant against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, but noted that the federal government doesn’t face the same constraints. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City can execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here. It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” Mamdani said in a video posted to X. “The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Advertisement During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani pledged to order Netanyahu’s arrest, and he recently told The New York Times that he was in active discussions with his office’s legal department about what the law would allow him to do. In the wake of that interview, President Donald Trump vowed that the Israeli leader, who could visit New York City this fall for the U.N. General Assembly, would not be arrested in the U.S. in “any way, shape, or form.” Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal. pic.twitter.com/YRezmW6YVx — Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani (@NYCMayor) July 22, 2026 Advertisement In 2024, the ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu and accused him of crimes against humanity, which he denied. That warrant stated that Netanyahu was “allegedly responsible for the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare and of intentionally directing an attack against the civilian population” as part of Israel’s military campaign against Gaza. The U.S. is not a party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and does not observe its authority. The Trump administration has also taken a more hostile posture toward the ICC, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently launching a campaign aimed at dismantling the body. Mamdani, who described Netanyahu as “the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” also detailed why the prime minister should be arrested. Advertisement “He is responsible for the killing of more than 73,000 people, for the maiming of tens of thousands of children, as those who survive undergo amputation without anesthesia. For the targeting of neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers, denying newborns even the chance to live. For the countless people he starved as he blocked food and humanitarian aid from reaching them. For the gunning down of hundreds of aid workers and journalists,” Mamdani alleged in his video on Tuesday. “And just last month, the U.N. confirmed that Palestinian children continue to be deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli armed forces more than eight months after the so-called ceasefire.” “I agree with the ICC that Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes, as I do for anyone else charged by the ICC,” Mamdani added. In a statement on Sunday, Netanyahu’s office attacked the ICC, calling it a “kangaroo court,” and criticized Mamdani in response. Advertisement “Mr. Mamdani should focus on fixing the damage his policies have caused New York,” the statement said. It also claimed that Israel had “taken unprecedented wartime measures to minimize harm to civilians while confronting Hamas.”

topic: Nuclear Deal Saudi Arabia Trump Administrations

left.title: US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium – US politics live

left-center.title: How the Trump Administration’s Nuclear Deal With Saudi Arabia May Work

center.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

right.title: Reports: Trump OKs Saudi Arabia Nuclear Deal

farLeftText: From 2h ago 17.21 EDT US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: double quotation mark The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Share Updated at 17.28 EDT 14m ago 18.41 EDT George Chidi At a Donald Trump event in Marietta, Georgia, the state’s Republican candidate for governor Rick Jackson pledged to ask the Georgia legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts, and if they failed to do so, would match the figure himself. “Here in Georgia, we should not stope with $1000,” Jackson said. “I believe our legislature will approve that. If they don’t I will personally donate it myself.” Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3 billion. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded a bit more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126 million in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15 billion in the bank, with $9 billion in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. Share 25m ago 18.31 EDT As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia, which borders Yemen, led a military intervention in 2015, triggering a year-long civil war to prop up Yemen’s Saudi-backed government. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations Centre said in an alert issued on Wednesday night it “has received a report of an incident 70NM southwest of AL SHUQAIQ, Saudi Arabia. The Master of a tanker reports being struck by an unknown projectile which has caused a fire onboard that the crew are currently fighting.” Senator Chris Murphy, a Connecticut Democrat, responded to news of the Houthi attacks by writing on social media: “Everyone saw all of this coming. This chaos was totally foreseeable and Trump chose to start this war anyway.” Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 39m ago 18.16 EDT Trump boasts about helping defeat Jon Ossoff in 2017 special election, fails to mention his role in Ossoff's Senate win in 2021 During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia on Wednesday, Donald Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff, the incumbent Democratic senator whose withering criticism of the president has thrilled Democrats and led to calls for Ossoff to mount a 2028 presidential campaign, should he first win his reelection bid in November. “I fought Ossoff before, as you know, and we won. We had a great success against this guy,” Trump said, fondly recalling his role in Ossoff’s narrow loss in a 2017 special election in Georgia’s sixth congressional district, in what was then a Republican-dominated Atlanta suburb. “He reminds me of Pinky Herman,” the president said. “Do you ever see Pinky Herman?” Trump asked, a comment that prompted some laughter from his supporters in the hall, and a scramble, among journalists, to figure out what, exactly, he was talking about. It was not until fully thirteen minutes later, when Trump predicted that Ossoff’s Republican challenger, Mike Collins, is “going to beat Pee-wee Herman”, that the 80-year-old president’s reference to the character played by the late comic actor Paul Reubens four decades ago became clear. For a president who rose to power largely through his insult-comic hits on his political opponents, it was a peculiarly low-energy jibe, given that Ossoff, who was once as skinny as the Reubens character, has now bulked up, and speaks about the president in a tone of righteous indignation that bears no resemblance to Pee-wee’s squeaky voice. Trump made no mention at all of the prominent role he played in trying, and failing, to stop Ossoff from winning his Senate seat in a run-off election on 5 January, 2021. The night before that election, in which Democratic senator Raphael Warnock also won his seat, Trump had traveled to Georgia to speak at a rally for the two Republican candidates, senators Kelly Loeffler and David Perdue. Rather than urging voters to support the Republican candidates, Trump instead undermined confidence in the electoral system in remarks focused mainly on his claim that the presidential election just two months earlier had been stolen from him. “Hello, Georgia! By the way, there is no way we lost Georgia. There’s no way, … That was a rigged election” Trump began his speech that night. “When you win in a landslide, and they steal it, and it’s rigged, it’s not acceptable,” he told the crowd, hours before Republican turnout slumped and Ossoff and Warnock were elected, swinging control of the Senate to the Democrats. View image in fullscreen Donald Trump with then-senator Kelly Loeffler at a rally in Dalton, Georgia on 4 January 2021, the night before Republicans lost a pair of Senate runoffs as Trump claimed that elections in the state were “rigged”, despite being administered by Republican officials. Photograph: Sandy Huffaker/AFP/Getty Images Share 2h ago 17.21 EDT US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: double quotation mark The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Share Updated at 17.28 EDT 2h ago 16.46 EDT Chris Stein The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with nearly all Democrats opposing its passage. The measure now goes to the Senate. Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military. “For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote. But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed. US House passes $1.15tn defense policy bill in near party-line vote Read more Share 2h ago 16.36 EDT Trump rails against specter of 'communism' to supporters in Georgia Donald Trump is currently delivering what is supposedly a presidential address to promote a new domestic policy, namely the investment accounts for children he calls Trump accounts, but, surrounded by supporters in Marietta, Georgia, his remarks quickly became deeply partisan, repeatedly attacking Democrats and falsely claiming the party is now filled with “communists”. Trump, whose political mentor Roy Cohn led senator Joseph McCarthy’s 1950s witch hunts for imaginary communist infiltrators in the US government, made the wild claim that Democrats he called “communists by another name” want to blow up Mount Rushmore. He then proclaimed: “we declare again today that the United States of America will never be a communist country!” The crowd, led by a group of die-hard Trump supporters who call themselves Front Row Joes, for their repeated presence at his rallies, broke into a chant of “USA! USA!” View image in fullscreen Donald Trump dances after speaking at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, on 22 July. Photograph: Saul Loeb/AFP/Getty Images Share Updated at 17.05 EDT 3h ago 15.57 EDT As I noted earlier, the parents of Lt Tyler Feehan accompanied Trump from Dover to Georgia aboard Air Force One, after the president attended a dignified transfer for four service members killed in the Middle East. Trump addressed Feehan’s family and their loss in his remarks today. “[Feehan] was a person that was going all the way. He was going to be a general. He would have been a general very quickly. He was heading in that path,” Trump said. Share 3h ago 15.41 EDT Donald Trump has started speaking in Marietta, Georgia. He started his speech joking that he was announces his candidacy for another term in office. Despite insisting that he was only “kidding”, it is something that the president has routinely floated – even though a third term would be in direct violation of the US constitution. Share Updated at 15.55 EDT 3h ago 15.38 EDT US measles cases hit 35-year high according to new data The number of measles cases in the US has hit a 35-year high according to new data released by Johns Hopkins University. According to the tracker, there have been 2,295 cases of measles in 2026, as of 21 July. This number is already higher than the 2,286 reported throughout the whole of 2025, and the highest level since 1991. The grim milestone is also the most cases reported in a single year since the disease was declared eliminated in the US in 2000. Unvaccinated individuals or those with unknown vaccination status represent almost all reported measles cases, according to Johns Hopkins. “High vaccination coverage has protected even the un- and undervaccinated because of herd protection, keeping measles at bay for the past few decades,” said William Moss, an epidemiology professor at Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health. “But in communities with pockets of susceptible individuals across a broad age range, measles has been able to take hold, infecting those who are susceptible and spreading rapidly.” “Measles vaccines are extremely safe and effective,” Moss added. “Getting you or your child vaccinated is the best way to protect against disease.” Share 4h ago 14.29 EDT Here's a recap of the day so far Ahead of Trump’s arrival in Georgia, here’s what’s happened today. On Capitol Hill, Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, defended the Trump administration’s unremitting “tariff spree” and insisted that prices for American families have not increased in the wake of the president’s duties on several countries. During a heated back and forth with Senator Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat from Massachusetts, Greer noted that “core inflation fell to 2.6 % year on year – much better than in January 2025.” While this metric excludes food and energy, overall inflation is actually around o.5% higher than it was when Trump took office. Trump attended the dignified transfer today of four US service members killed in the Middle East. It was the third ceremony that the president has attended since he launched the war in Iran in February. Speaking to reporters on his way to Dover, Trump called it “one of the hardest things to do as a president.” His message to grieving families, he said, will simply be “we love you, we love your child”. Earlier, the president took to Truth Social and noted that the US will destroy one bridge or power plant, including those near Tehran, “any time” that Iran “shoots a ship” in the strait of Hormuz. This comes after the US conducted its 11th consecutive day of strikes on Iran, with the war showing no sign if easing. Senate Republicans have also released the text for the Clarity act today – the legislation that would ban public officials from issuing or sponsoring cryptocurrency or digital assets . The bill’s guardrails would apply to the president, vice-president, members of Congress, federal judges and their spouses, and wouldn’t expire until January 2029 – the end of Trump’s term. The bill is one of the first major attempts to shape cryptocurrency use for those in office, and comes after the president’s financial disclosures revealed the president took in more than $1bn from his crypto businesses since returning to the White House. Meanwhile, in the House, Republican speaker Mike Johnson is desperate to pass the framework for a budget bill that would guarantee funding for the war with Iran. The legislation, which GOP leaders are hoping to pass by reconciliation and avoid the Senate filibuster, faces an array of challenges, including from fiscal hawks who complain that its expected $95bn cost is not offset by any spending cuts, and other Republicans upset that it does not answer the Trump administration’s request for an extra $350bn in defense spending. Johnson can only afford to lose two votes in his quest to pass the package. Share Updated at 16.00 EDT 5h ago 13.54 EDT As we noted earlier, Trump is now headed to Marietta, Georgia, where he’ll deliver remarks at a local high school at 3pm ET. We’ll bring you the latest as that gets under way. In the meantime, a White House official says that the family of 1Lt Tyler James Feehan, one of the US soldiers killed during Iranian strikes in Jordan, will be flying with the president and his team. Feehan’s family are from Georgia, the official notes, and the president “offered to give them a ride home”. Share 5h ago 13.33 EDT Senate Republicans have also released the text for the Clarity act today – the legislation that would ban public officials from issuing or sponsoring cryptocurrency or digital assets. The bill’s guardrails would apply to the president, vice-president, members of Congress, federal judges and their spouses, and wouldn’t expire until January 2029 – the end of Donald Trump’s term. It also requires officials to either sell their crypto holdings and investments in crypto companies or place them in a blind trust they are not able to control. The bill is one of the first major attempts to shape cryptocurrency use for those in office. A reminder that Trump took in more than $1bn from his crypto businesses since returning to the White House, according to recent financial disclosures. They showed the president earned more than $500m from his World Liberty Financial business selling new crypto products, while another crypto business, CIC Digital LLC, took in more than $600m from sales of souvenir-type “meme” coins stamped with Trump’s face that launched days before his inauguration. Share Updated at 14.30 EDT 6h ago 13.08 EDT Also today, top Democrat Chuck Schumer plans to seek unanimous consent to pass his Presidential Airlift Security Act, which would stop the Pentagon from spending more to modify, restore, or maintain the Boeing 747-8 gifted by the government of Qatar, which Donald Trump has started to use as the new Air Force One. Republicans previously blocked the legislation, introduced by the Senate minority leader, last year. On Sunday, Trump admitted that his jet – valued at close to $400m – cannot match the defensive armor of a true presidential aircraft, telling reporters it will soon be grounded and “maxed out” with the protections it was never built with. This comes after the New York Times reported that the new plane lacks defensive systems fitted to the purpose-built Air Force One fleet, including infrared countermeasures to divert heat-seeking missiles and sensors to detect incoming launches. The president flew the jet to Ankara, Turkey for the Nato summit earlier this month but switched to an older, more heavily defended aircraft for his return to the US – at the advice of the Secret Service. Share 6h ago 12.51 EDT Earlier today, ahead of attending a dignified transfer of US service members who died in the Middle East, Trump claimed that a new poll just claimed that while “Americans don’t want high gasoline prices but they’re not against the war”. However, the president didn’t elaborate on who published the new data. For context, a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll actually found that only 29% of Americans approve of Trump’s conduct in overseeing the war with Iran. Share
