topic: Tropical Storm Bertha Gulf Coast Impacting

left-center.title: Tropical Storm Bertha impacting northern Gulf Coast. See its path.

center.title: Tropical Storm Bertha forecast to bring heavy rain, flooding along the Gulf Coast

right-center.title: Beaches closed as Tropical Storm Bertha approaches Gulf Coast

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
Tropical Storm Bertha was bringing rain to large swaths of the northern Gulf Coast early Wednesday as its projected path sparked watches and warnings.

Bertha was moving westward at 5 mph and that general motion was expected to continue through Friday "with little acceleration," the National Hurricane Center said.

"On the forecast track," the Miami-based center said, "Bertha will move near or along the northern Gulf Coast during the next couple of days" and "gradual weakening is forecast to continue."

Bertha was packing maximum sustained winds of 50 mph, down 10 mph from Tuesday night.

Map shows projected path

Bertha, the second named storm of the Atlantic hurricane season, was forecast to stick close to the coast.

As of 5 a.m. EDT, Bertha was about 165 miles west-southwest of Panama City, Fla. and some 50 miles east-northeast of the mouth of the Mississippi River, the hurricane center pointed out.

CBS News

Warnings and watches in effect

A tropical storm warning was posted Wednesday from the Okaloosa/Walton County Line in Florida to Morgan City, Louisiana as well as for New Orleans and Lake Pontchartrain.

A tropical storm watch was up for the area west of Morgan City to Cameron, Louisiana, and Lake Maurepas.

"Tropical storm conditions will continue spreading across portions of the northern Gulf Coast within the warning area today. Tropical storm conditions are possible within the watch area in Louisiana by tonight," the center said.

"Bertha is expected to produce total rainfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches with isolated higher totals to 6 inches through Thursday

along the central to the western Gulf Coast from western Florida to southern Texas," the center noted. "This rainfall may produce isolated flash flooding, especially in urban areas."

The system could also produce storm surge, raising waters up to 4 feet in some locations along the coast.

Bertha comes weeks after the 2026 hurricane season's first named storm, Tropical Storm Arthur, which made landfall in June along the Gulf Coast of Texas.
centerText:
Tropical Storm Bertha strengthened in the Gulf of Mexico on Tuesday but was expected to weaken as it chugs closer to the U.S., potentially bringing coastal flooding and heavy rain from the Florida Panhandle to Louisiana.

READ MORE: Tropical Storm Bertha forms along U.S. Gulf Coast

Those areas along the northern Gulf Coast were under a tropical storm warning through Wednesday and could see rain and flooding into Thursday morning, the Miami-based National Hurricane Center said. New Orleans also was under a tropical storm warning.

The storm was about 105 miles (170 kilometers) southwest of Panama City, Florida, and roughly the same distance southeast of Mobile, Alabama. It was moving slowly to the northwest Tuesday afternoon with maximum sustained winds of 60 mph (95 kph), the hurricane center said Tuesday. Gradual weakening was forecast through Friday.

Bertha was forecast to make landfall Wednesday night somewhere in the coastal and lightly populated part of Louisiana southeast of New Orleans, said Michael Buchanan, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service in Louisiana.

Wind will be biggest threat there, he said.

Authorities open sites to distribute sandbags

Authorities in Mississippi, Louisiana and Florida opened sites to provide sandbags to residents while red flag warnings were posted along some beaches advising swimmers about the dangers of rip tides.

Up and down the coast, double red flags signaled that Gulf waters were closed for swimming.

Beaches near Pensacola, Florida, were mostly deserted except for tourists venturing out to take photos of the pounding surf.

"For locals, this is just another storm," said Candice Henderson, manager of the Crab Trap, a beachside restaurant that was packed Tuesday on Pensacola's Perdido Key. "When you're on vacation and its raining, you either shop or you go out to eat."

The storm was expected to weaken as it moves along the coast, said Jamie Rhome of the hurricane center. It's possible that the worst of the storm will stay offshore, he said Tuesday.

Bertha was expected to weaken further as it pushes into Texas, forecasters said.

Forecasts called for as much as 2 to 4 inches (5 to 10 centimeters) of rain from western Florida to the southern Louisiana coastline, with higher amounts in some scattered areas.

That could cause flash flooding in some urban areas.

Authorities also advised that areas in Louisiana from the mouth of the Mississippi River to the Alabama-Mississippi state line could see storm surges of up to 4 feet (1.2 meters). Parts of Florida and Alabama could see smaller surges.

The tropical storm warning extended west to Morgan City, Louisiana.

Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season

Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season. Tropical Storm Arthur last month brought heavy rainfall to the southeastern U.S.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration expects this year to see a below-average Atlantic hurricane season although that doesn't eliminate the chances of a catastrophic storm.

Bertha could dump heavy rain on coastal areas of Mississippi that are still recovering from flooding brought by Arthur that destroyed and damaged homes in June.

"Because of that, people are definitely paying attention, or at least you would hope so," said Jeff Clark, a spokesperson for Mississippi's Harrison County, where six sites were open for sandbag distribution.

So far, there hasn't been a need to open any shelters or order evacuations, Clark said on Tuesday.

Hurricane Fausto churns in the Pacific

In the eastern Pacific Ocean, Hurricane Fausto churned far from Mexico's Baja California peninsula and posed no threat to land, the hurricane center said.

Fausto gained hurricane status Monday night. It was about 780 miles (1,260 kilometers) southwest of the southern tip of Baja California, had top sustained winds of 75 mph (120 kph) and was moving west-northwest at 12 mph (19 kph).

The storm was expected to strengthen Tuesday and Wednesday. No coastal watches or warnings were in effect, though ocean swells generated by the storm could cause life-threatening surf and rip tide conditions on the Baja California coast, the center said.

Associated Press journalists Kimberly Chandler in Montgomery, Alabama, Stephen Smith in New Orleans, and Haya Panjwani in Washington contributed.

centerText: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authority" to do so. In a video posted on social media, Mamdani instead called on US officials to act on the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for the Israeli Prime Minister over alleged war crimes. Technically, if Netanyahu set foot in an ICC member state, he could be arrested and handed over to the court - but the US is not a member of the ICC. President Donald Trump has said Netanyahu would not be "arrested, in any way, shape or form" while in the US. "He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors [sic], and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others," Trump said in a Truth Social post on Monday. Trump was responding to comments Mamdani made in an interview with the New York Times on Saturday, in which the mayor said he was having "an active conversation" with law enforcement about the possibility of enforcing the ICC arrest warrant. On Tuesday, Mamdani said his team had reviewed "every avenue available" under the law, and concluded that "we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this". "The federal government, however, does," Mamdani stated. "And I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant." Mamdani said in the social media video that Netanyahu was "not welcome" in New York City, where he is expected to travel in September for the UN General Assembly. The ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, while also issuing an arrest warrant for a Hamas commander. The ICC has the authority to prosecute those accused of genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes on the territory of states party to the Rome Statute, its founding treaty. "I believe that Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague," Mamdani said during his New York Times interview, referencing the UN's International Court of Justice. "He's a war criminal who has been charged by the International Criminal Court." During his campaign for New York City mayor, Mamdani repeatedly called Netanyahu a war criminal and has called the war in Gaza a genocide. His remarks come as Democratic voters' support for Israel appears to be shifting, with a recent poll , externalsuggesting 58% of Democrats believe the US is too supportive of Israel, up from 45% in 2024. In response to Mamdani's interview, Danny Danon, the Israeli ambassador to the UN said to the mayor: "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda."
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani declined Wednesday to say when he learned the city had no authority to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, after repeatedly campaigning on a promise to detain the Israeli leader if he came to New York. Pressed over whether the now-abandoned arrest threat amounted to "political theater," Mamdani said his administration had reviewed the applicable laws and concluded that only the federal government could execute the International Criminal Court warrant. "It’s clear that the city does not have the authority to execute this warrant," Mamdani admitted at a New York City Hall news conference Wednesday, urging the federal government to act on the ICC warrant even though the U.S. is not a member of the ICC and does not recognize the court’s jurisdiction in the U.S. or over Israel, which is also not a member. With the Trump administration already fully rejecting his call to arrest Netanyahu for being a "war criminal," Mamdani called for New Yorkers to take to protest "if anyone feels a level of frustration or opposition." MAMDANI GOES BACK ON CAMPAIGN PROMISE, SAYS NYC CAN'T ARREST NETANYAHU "I believe that this is an arrest warrant that should be executed, and also that I would follow the laws that we had, and I would be respectful of those laws," he added. "And that is why I asked my administration to review of all applicable laws. And I believe, however, in that same finding, that the United States government does have the ability to both joining the International Criminal Court and executing that work, and that is something that I want to see happen." Short of that, Mamdani suggested that people protest when the Israeli leader visits for the United Nations General Assembly, potentially in September. "This is a city where, if anyone feels a level of frustration or opposition, protest is something that we will always respect," Mamdani told reporters. "I leave it to New Yorkers themselves to make that decision." The mayor was repeatedly asked why he did not resolve the legal question before taking office, given that experts had challenged the legality of his arrest pledge during the campaign. HAMAS, HEZBOLLAH FLAGS FLY AT ANTI-ISRAEL PROTEST MINUTES AFTER MAMDANI CONDEMNS TERROR PLOT AGAINST JEWS Mamdani would not provide a date for when his administration informed him that the city lacked the power to carry out the ICC warrant. "That’s a review that I asked them to do," he said. "They made it clear that this is not possible through applicable law." Mamdani said Palestinian New Yorkers disappointed that he cannot fulfill his arrest promise had reason to be frustrated, declaring that Netanyahu would no longer be welcomed by City Hall. "I understand it because we are talking about someone who has been charged with crimes against humanity, someone who has visited the city on many an occasion and who has been welcomed by previous mayors," Mamdani said. "I want to make it very clear that that welcome ends with me," he continued. "I will not be welcoming Benjamin Netanyahu or any other war criminals when they come to this city." NYC REPUBLICAN LAWMAKER SLAMS MAMDANI ANTISEMITISM OFFICE AS A ‘BLACK HOLE’ LACKING PUBLIC-FACING RESOURCES Mamdani also faced questions over criticism that his focus on Netanyahu created a one-sided account of the Israel-Hamas war. Asked directly whether he considered Hamas’ Oct. 7, 2023, terrorist attack to be genocide, Mamdani did not use that term. "I consider Oct. 7 to be a horrific war crime," he said. "I’ve considered it to be something that should be deeply opposed with every single fiber of our being." Mamdani said his approach was rooted in what he called the "universality of humanity," arguing that officials should not "pick and choose which crimes against humanity we oppose." The mayor also said he would support honoring ICC warrants against Hamas leaders as well as Netanyahu. "My interest in executing that warrant is not specific to Benjamin Netanyahu, but to anyone who they put a warrant towards for war crimes and crimes against humanity," Mamdani said. He accused Netanyahu of having "orchestrated this genocide" in Gaza and described the Israeli prime minister as a "war criminal." Israel has repeatedly rejected genocide allegations and condemned the ICC case as illegitimate. "My responsibility to them is to ensure that they are cherished, they are celebrated and they are safe in their city," he said, "and that never are they made to feel as if their opinions on any one issue is the precondition for that safety."

right-center.title: Nolan Wells independent autopsy doesn't point to foul play, former medical examiner says

centerText: CHICAGO (AP) — An independent autopsy commissioned by the family of Nolan Xavier Wells found the 18-year-old's cause and manner of death to be "undetermined" pending further investigation. Watch the news conference live in the video player above. The civil rights attorney Ben Crump, who is representing the Wells family, announced the preliminary findings Wednesday in front of a standing-room-only crowd at the annual NAACP National Convention in Chicago. READ MORE: What to know about Nolan Wells' independent autopsy results Reading from a report prepared by forensic pathologist Dr. Robert Mitchell, Crump said the report showed no evidence of fractures or bruising, but also did not rule out the possibility of foul play. The board-certified pathologist found "red discoloration" in the soft tissue in the back of Wells' head, but no underlying skull fractures or lacerations to the skin around the area, Crump said. He noted that the decomposition of the body, along with other limiting factors, had made it impossible to make a determination. Without further information, the pathologist wrote he "cannot rule out if any intervening nonaccidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells," according to the attorney. READ MORE: Nolan Wells remembered for his gentle spirit as community demands answers about his death The autopsy is part of an independent investigation led by Crump into the circumstances of the teenager's disappearance, which followed a July 4 boat trip with friends to an island off the coast of Mississippi. Wells did not return to the mainland with his friends. His body was found floating off the island's coast two days later. The Mississippi State Medical Examiner's Office conducted its own autopsy but has yet to release the results. WATCH: Nolan Wells' family presses for transparent investigation into his death The second autopsy was arranged by the family and Crump, and paid for by former NFL player Colin Kaepernick. It did not include a toxicology report. "This is only another part of the puzzle, and even though it tells us significant information, we still have more questions than we have answers at this time," Crump said of the results. READ MORE: What we know about death of Nolan Wells, Black teen who went missing on Mississippi boat trip The still-unknown circumstances around Wells' death have touched off rampant online speculation and prompted calls from Black leaders for transparency from local authorities. Wells, who was Black, had traveled to the island with a group of high school friends, all of whom are white. A reward of $125,000 — jointly funded by the Rev. Al Sharpton, actor and producer Tyler Perry and former NFL player Terrell Owens — is now being offered in exchange for information leading to an arrest and conviction in connection with the death. The autopsy announcement comes two days after Wells was laid to rest in his hometown of Ocean Springs, Mississippi. In his eulogy, Sharpton lamented the "inconsistencies and unanswered questions" that had placed a dark cloud over the grieving family. He questioned why Wells' friends had left him behind, taken his phone and keys and failed to report him missing. And he suggested local investigators had spoken prematurely in statements to the news media indicating no crime was suspected. "The reason that a lot of people across the nation are looking at this Nolan situation is because we're looking at the history in Mississippi," Sharpton added. "We can't have someone in law enforcement saying there was no foul play before there was an investigation." Jackson County Sheriff John Ledbetter said initial interviews suggested Wells chose to stay on the island and that his friends believed he would get a ride with someone else. He has said the friends were cooperating with the investigation, which remains ongoing. "We are trying to be respectful and collaborative as much as we can," Crump said. "But it's always troublesome to all of us when these investigations just take forever."
centerRightText:
NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

Attorney Ben Crump revealed that the results of an independent autopsy into Nolan Wells' death are "inconclusive" as a former medical examiner said the findings aren't consistent with a "cover up" or any wrongdoing.

Crump announced the independent autopsy's results on Wednesday at the NAACP National Convention in Chicago, stating that an area of "red discoloration" was found on Wells' head. He said Dr. Roger Mitchell, who performed the autopsy, couldn't come to a conclusion "pending further investigation." The official autopsy hasn't yet been completed.

"Why is this dark red here in the back of his head?" Crump said. "This pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells."

When asked by Fox News Digital about the case, forensic pathologist Dr. Michael Baden said the head injury doesn't point to foul play.

"So that could be from bumping into something in the water. There's a lot of debris in the water, for example. It depends how deep it is, but it's describing a really superficial impact that could have happened before death or after death," Baden said.

SEARCH CALLED OFF AFTER BODY MATCHING MISSING TEEN NOLAN WELLS FOUND OFF MISSISSIPPI COAST

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

"What he's describing is a minor injury that could be... totally innocent," Baden said.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Baden said Mitchell was "reaching for straws" in the independent autopsy report, adding that the findings are consistent with accounts from local law enforcement that his death was accidental and no foul play was involved.

GOT A TIP?

Wells, 18, initially disappeared July 4 after he went with friends on a boat trip to Horn Island, which is a barrier island off Mississippi's Gulf Coast. Officials and search crews searched for two days before a National Park Service ranger located his body on July 6 while on the island's northwestern tip.

Officials haven't released a cause of death as officials continue to investigate.

topic: Trump Dignified Transfer Attends Service Members

left-center.title: Trump attends dignified transfer of 4 service members killed in Iran attacks since the ceasefire ended

right-center.title: Trump flies family of fallen service member home to Georgia after dignified transfer

right.title: Trump Attends Dignified Transfer Ceremony for 4 'Great Heroes' — 'One of the Hardest Things to Do'

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: President Donald Trump on Wednesday attended the dignified transfer of the remains of four service members killed in Iranian attacks this month after hostilities ramped up following the collapse of the ceasefire. Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content. The four service members being flown from the Middle East to Dover Air Force Base were 28-year-old Angel S. Rampersad of New York, 30-year-old Michael Emmanuel Swinton of North Carolina, 25-year-old Tyler James Feehan of Hawaii and 19-year-old Isabella Gonzales of Texas. Dignified transfers are some of the most solemn occasions for presidents, who often attend to pay respects to troops killed in conflicts overseen by them as commander in chief. Trump has attended two prior dignified transfers since the war began in February, but this is his first since he declared on July 8 that the ceasefire was over. President Donald Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth salute as members of a U.S. Army carry team move a transfer case Wednesday. Kevin Dietsch / Getty Images The event marked the latest impact of the war, which has garnered criticism from lawmakers on both sides of the aisle who question the administration’s strategy in the monthslong conflict. The hostilities, which were paused after the two sides agreed to a peace plan that quickly fell apart, have thrown the region into chaos, leaving at least 18 Americans dead, costing at least $37.5 billion and inflating the price of oil. The president and his administration have vigorously defended their handling of the war, arguing it is necessary to prevent Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon. Trump told reporters earlier Wednesday: “We’re going to Dover. Some of you are going with us. To honor our heroes, and they are indeed great heroes, actually. They said, and all of them said very strongly, we can’t let, we cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon. They won’t have a nuclear weapon. So we’re going to honor them. And it’s one of the hardest things to do, for me, it’s one of the hardest things to do as a president, but has to be done.” The family members of the fallen service members attended the dignified transfer, where the bodies of their loved ones were moved in American flag-draped transfer cases from a plane into waiting vans. Uniformed service members marched the transfer cases past officials, including Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt. Sens. Thom Tillis, R-N.C., and Chris Coons, D-Del., also attended. Trump and other officials saluted as the remains passed before them, while others placed their hands on their hearts. The president also met privately with the families, the White House confirmed. From left, Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, Sgt. Angel Rampersad, Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton. DVIDS Flag-draped transfer cases are loaded into vehicles Wednesday at Dover Air Force Base. Kevin Dietsch / Getty Images Gonzales was killed Friday and Feehan was killed Saturday, the Pentagon said. It also said that Swinton was killed Sunday “during a controlled detonation” of an attack drone. Rampersad was “believed to have been killed” in Jordan, the Pentagon said, adding that “the incident is under investigation.” The Pentagon has said that she is believed to have been the missing soldier who is presumed dead. Hegseth faced questions from senators Tuesday about the cost and strategy of the war during a tense hearing on the Pentagon’s budget request. During one particularly heated exchange, Sen. Gary Peters, D-Mich., called the Trump administration’s efforts “an utter and complete failure” and pressed Hegseth on what the long-term plan is to end the war. “You have over a trillion dollars,” Peters said. “You’re asking for one and a half trillion dollars. It’s not a question of money. It’s a question of leadership and understanding what it takes to actually win.”

centerRightText:
The family of one of the fallen U.S. service members from the war in Iran accompanied President Donald Trump to Georgia after they received his flag-draped body during a somber dignified transfer process.

The family of 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Hawaii, received a standing ovation during Trump’s campaign rally at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, an Atlanta suburb, when he acknowledged they were part of the crowd.

Trump had earlier attended the dignified transfer at Dover Air Base, Delaware, of Feehan and three other service members who died last week in Jordan and Iraq as the United States and Iran exchange an escalation of strikes in the aftermath of the cessation of their memorandum of understanding.

That’s their child, and all you can do is throw out your heart.” Advertisement As we reported Monday, the president and Secretary of State Marco Rubio honored the first two soldiers who were confirmed to have been killed in an Iranian missile strike on an American base in Jordan, 19-year-old Pvt. Isabella Gonzales of Carrollton, Texas, and 25-year-old 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan of Hawaii. We now also know that New York’s Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad (28) also died during that attack, while North Carolina’s Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton (30) perished due to a drone detonation in Iraq. Today, we honor four American heroes who made the ultimate sacrifice in service to our nation. 1st Lt. Tyler Feehan Sgt. Michael Swinton Sgt. Angel Rampersad Pvt. Isabella Gonzales May God hold them in His eternal embrace and comfort their grieving families, and may God forever… pic.twitter.com/P5NA59ltAU — Rapid Response 47 (@RapidResponse47) July 22, 2026 Today, we honor four American heroes who made the ultimate sacrifice in service to our nation. 1st Lt. Tyler Feehan Sgt. Michael Swinton Sgt. Angel Rampersad Pvt. Isabella Gonzales May God hold them in His eternal embrace and comfort their grieving families, and may God forever bless the United States of America. 🙏🇺🇸 'Great Patriots': Trump, Rubio Honor Two Young US Troops Killed by Iranian Missile Attacks Advertisement CENTCOM Reports 2 US Fatalities in Iranian Attack on Jordan; SecWar Hegseth Reacts The military describes a dignified transfer as a solemn procedure, not a ceremony: A dignified transfer is the process by which, upon the return from the theater of operations to the United States, the remains of fallen military members are transferred from the aircraft to an awaiting vehicle. The remains will then be transferred to the mortuary facility located at Air Force Mortuary Affairs Operations, Dover AFB, Del. The dignified transfer is not a ceremony; rather, it is a solemn movement of the transfer case by a carry team composed of military personnel from the fallen member's respective service. topic: House Shutdown Passes Spending Government Midterms

left.title: House passes spending bill to head off government shutdown before midterms

left-center.title: House Narrowly Passes Stopgap Spending Bill, but Shutdown Fight Looms

right-center.title: House GOP votes to punt government shutdown battle to after November midterms

farLeftText:
The US House of Representatives on Tuesday approved a stopgap measure to head off a government shutdown until early December, with its Republican leaders seeking to defuse a volatile issue in the run-up to the November midterm elections.

The continuing resolution authorizing government spending until 4 December passed by a vote of 220-205, roughly along party lines. It next heads to the Senate, where it will need to win bipartisan support to clear the chamber.

The government’s current spending authority expires on 30 September, but top Republicans had argued the measure’s passage was necessary to ensure that Democrats do not block government funding measures in the Senate.

They did so last year for a record 43 days in protest against the expiration of tax credits that lowered some healthcare costs, and twice again this year, to push for reforms to immigration enforcement operations after the killings of two US citizens in Minneapolis.

“Our conference refuses to let the obstruction and inaction of Senate Democrats trigger another manufactured shutdown at the end of September. That is why we are acting before a funding lapse crisis, not in response to it,” said Tom Cole, the House appropriations committee chair who spearheaded the resolution.

Earlier on Tuesday, lawmakers agreed to move forward on the stopgap measure and a budget framework paving the way for a $95bn party-line package, in a 214-211 vote roughly along party lines.

The constitution tasks Congress with authorizing government spending, and the Senate and House every year attempt to pass 12 appropriations measures granting those authorities. But lawmakers often miss those deadlines, forcing them to resort to passing continuing resolutions that extend governmental departments’ ability to operate.

While the House has passed three appropriations measures for the 2027 fiscal year beginning on 1 October, the Senate – where the top Democrat and Republican say they remain far apart in government spending talks – has approved none, nor have any advanced through committees, a key procedural step. Republican leaders ‌in the chamber said they were negotiating with Senate Democrats on their own short-term funding bill.

Rosa DeLauro, the Democratic ranking member on the House appropriations committee, denied that her party was seeking a shutdown, and called passage of a stopgap funding measure “necessary”.

However, she opposed the Republican-backed measure, calling it “hastily” conceived and noting it appropriated funds for the border patrol without “substantial reforms” to the agency that plays a major role in Donald Trump’s crackdown on undocumented immigrants.

“We are putting the cart before the horse by considering this legislation without understanding the full scope of what is needed to continue operations through December 4,” DeLauro said.

Should the Senate fail to pass the measure, funding negotiations will intensify from September, when Congress returns to Washington DC after spending most of August in recess with the end-of-the-month deadline to prevent a shutdown near.

Those talks could be complicated by the approaching midterm elections, in which Democrats hope they can retake control of the House and potentially the Senate, where they can only win a majority by picking up at least three seats in states that backed Trump two years ago.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerRightText:
NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

The House of Representatives voted on Tuesday night to punt the threat of another government shutdown until after the November midterm elections.

House lawmakers passed a bill to extend the current fiscal year's funding levels until Dec. 4 via a measure called a continuing resolution (CR). It would extend several weeks into fiscal year (FY) 2027, which begins on Oct. 1.

The legislation passed in a 220 to 205 vote. Just six House Democrats supported the measure compared to 204 who voted against. All but one House Republican supported the bill.

It's a victory for Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., and House Republicans, who are looking to avoid a messy federal spending fight right before what's expected to be an uphill battle to retain their razor-thin majority.

SENATE WEIGHS NEW, PAINFUL LEVERAGE TACTIC AS FEARS OF ANOTHER GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN GROW

House Majority Whip Tom Emmer, R-Minn., the No. 3 House GOP leader, ripped the Democrats who opposed the measure.

"As the marxists and communists battle it out for control of the Democrat party, House Republicans are proving yet again that we are the party that governs and works for the American people. Under President Trump’s leadership, we are the party of commonsense. The other side — led by Commie Mamdani — is the party of chaos and destruction. A majority of Democrats just voted for another damning Schumer Shutdown," he said in reference to New York City's self-proclaimed Democratic Socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

The current fiscal year saw the longest government shutdown in history, lasting from Oct. 1, 2025 until Nov. 12, 2025, as Senate Democrats blockaded GOP-led funding priorities for weeks in protest of spending cuts to federal healthcare programs.

And Republicans are warning that Democrats would almost certainly cause another government shutdown right before the election if the fight is not punted until after the early November midterms.

"Unfortunately, given that Democrats have already forced the two longest government shutdowns in American history in this Congress, one of them for 78 days, they've already demonstrated they don't care what kind of pain they inflict on the American people," Johnson told reporters on Tuesday. "They've shown there's nothing they'd love more than a government shutdown as tens of millions of Americans head to the polls in November."

House Democratic Caucus Chairman Pete Aguilar, D-Calif., told reporters Tuesday that he would vote against the bill along with the top Democrat on the House Appropriations Committee, the panel tasked with overseeing yearly federal spending.

"I think that there are some drafting errors within the CR that give Customs and Border Patrol flexibility to move money around. The administration said that all of that was funded within reconciliation 2.0. and I think they're creating other opportunities to transfer funds," Aguilar said.

But even with the House's victory, it's not clear that the fight will be over before September.

HOUSE GOP PUSHES BACK ON SENATE'S 'SKINNY' PLAN TO END RECORD-BREAKING DHS SHUTDOWN

The GOP-led Senate is working on its own course on funding extension, with sources familiar with the process telling Fox News Digital that appropriators were crafting their own CR. They believe the House’s product lacked key extensions to certain programs that are expected to run out of funding come the Sept. 30 deadline.

Those programs, known as anomalies, were shipped to the Senate from the White House on Monday.

Sen. John Hoeven, R-N.D., a member of the Senate Appropriations Committee, told Fox News Digital that Republicans and Democrats, for the time being, were working together to thwart a shutdown.

He hoped to get the CR onto the floor as soon as this week, given that the House is set to leave Washington, D.C., by the end of the week for a long August recess.

"Look, we appreciate the House moving forward with theirs. It's, you know, very clean," Hoeven said. "There are just a few issues that we think need to be the rest. Because, obviously, we need to get Democrats on board with us, right now, they're working with us."

CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP

The move has the full blessing of Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D.

"I think it's going to be really important that we figure out a path forward to get a bipartisan agreement to fund the government that will keep government open and ensure that government employees are getting paid," Thune said. "And that is something that I intend to bring to the floor for a vote before the August break.

topic: 2028 South Carolina Nevada Democrats Presidential

left-center.title: South Carolina and Nevada Fight to Kick Off Democrats’ 2028 Presidential Calendar

center.title: Democrats could take initial vote on 2028 presidential primary calendar this week

right-center.title: Nevada and South Carolina battle in DNC to be first 2028 primary state

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
South Carolina and Nevada have emerged as the two front-runners to begin the 2028 Democratic presidential nominating calendar, but their jockeying has grown acrimonious and pitted Black and Latino party leaders against each other, according to interviews with more than a dozen party officials involved in the discussions.

A powerful panel of 49 insiders of the Democratic National Committee is set to meet this week in Washington to sketch out the order of the early-state calendar. The full party body would then vote next month to finalize the choices.

The order of primary states is deeply consequential. Different states have different upsides and downsides for the party’s potential 2028 contenders — and require the constructing of different political coalitions. The calendar is likely to play a significant role in deciding who ultimately emerges as the next Democratic presidential nominee. And months of private lobbying, backroom discussions and brass-knuckle racial politics are bursting into the open on the eve of the gathering.

“It’s almost like we’re running against Nevada,” Christale Spain, the chair of the South Carolina Democratic Party, said in an interview. “That’s what it feels like.”
centerText:
The Rules and Bylaws Committee has been holding monthly meetings as it considers the order in which states will vote during the 2028 primaries. The panel has heard presentations from a dozen states hoping to kick off the party's primaries and caucuses, including Iowa, Michigan, New Hampshire, Nevada and South Carolina.

The panel that sets the party's voting order could make that determination as early as Friday when it meets in Washington, two people with knowledge of the party's process but not authorized to discuss it publicly told The Associated Press on Monday.

COLUMBIA, S.C. (AP) — Democrats could vote as early as this week on the order of their primary calendar for the 2028 presidential election, although the decision wouldn't be final until the full Democratic National Committee agrees.

The calendar isn't considered set until it's approved by the full DNC, which is scheduled to meet in August. The DNC did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Democrats upended their process for setting their calendar after Iowa's 2020 Democratic caucus debacle, which led to days of uncertainty over who had won. The party since then has favored more diverse states.

For the 2024 cycle, President Joe Biden led a DNC effort to have South Carolina — which for years has held the party's first Southern contest — go first overall in the party's primaries. He cited the state's more racially diverse population compared to the traditional first-in-the-nation states of Iowa and New Hampshire, which are overwhelmingly white.

Four years earlier, Rep. Jim Clyburn, South Carolina's lone congressional Democrat and for a time the top Black Democratic lawmaker on Capitol Hill, helped Biden revive his flagging primary campaign and win a resounding victory in the state. He then went on to secure the party's presidential nomination.

South Carolina's Democratic leaders have pushed for the state to lead off again, lobbying for support and getting buy-in from Democratic Party chairs in five Southern states. In May, they pressed for South Carolina to reprise its role as the party's first-in-the-nation contest holder, arguing that the state best represents the initial playing field for presidential candidates to build the coalitions needed to win.

A quarter of the state's population is Black and about 9% is Hispanic, according to the Census Bureau.
centerRightText:
Nevada is angling to oust South Carolina as the first state to hold the Democratic primary in the 2028 elections, a move that is causing a ruckus in the caucus.

South Carolina was first on Democrats’ primary calendar in 2024, when the state played a critical part in nominating then-President Joe Biden for a second term. Some Democrats favor the state to come in first due to its sizable black population, as the demographic is considered a key component of the party’s power.

However, Nevada is now angling to lead the country’s Democratic primaries, as a panel of 49 Democratic National Committee insiders is set to meet this week in Washington, D.C., to sketch out the order of the early-state calendar, according to the New York Times. The development comes as Nevada party leaders believe its swing-state status and large Hispanic population would make Democrats more electable in the general election, arguing that it should push it to first-place status in the primaries, over South Carolina, a solidly red state.

Advertisement

topic: Beluga Whales Park Closed Canadian Theme

left-center.title: Inside the transfer of beluga whales from a closed Canadian theme park to U.S. aquariums

center.title: Watch: Beluga whales travel by plane and lorry to US from closed Canadian marine park

right-center.title: Beluga whales from shuttered Canada theme park arrive at SeaWorld San Antonio

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
Six beluga whales have arrived at aquariums in the United States after being moved from a closed Canadian marine park that could no longer care for the mammals.

They are part of a large pod of 30 belugas living at Marineland of Canada that need to be rehomed.

Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lillooet arrived at a Chicago facility, while Bertie Botts and Frankie arrived at SeaWorld in San Antonio, Texas.

Transported in specialised cold-water containers by truck and plane, the animals were accompanied throughout the journey by marine mammal veterinarians. This is the first stage of an unprecedented international relocation effort.

The move followed months of planning and veterinary checks, with specialist teams now monitoring the whales as they settle into their new homes.
centerRightText:
See more of our coverage in your search results.

This rescue was no fluke.

The first group of beluga whales rescued from a shuttered Canadian theme park has arrived safely at their new home in Texas.

The belugas named Bertie Botts and Frankie, from Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, traveled by truck and plane to reach their new abode at SeaWorld San Antonio, the theme park announced in a statement on Tuesday.

Photos captured the ginormous marine mammals with their adorable flippers flapping to the side as they were lowered into their new pool habitats in huge slings.

3 Bertie Botts and Frankie, the first group of beluga whales rescued from a shuttered Canadian theme park, have arrived safely at SeaWorld San Antonio. Shedd Aquarium/AFP via Getty Images

Aquatic medicine specialists, veterinarians and caretakers accompanied the majestic sea canaries on their international voyage, sometimes even joining them in the water.

Six whales were moved during the first transfer from the defunct marine park — the beginning of relocation efforts for all 30 of the whales.

Sierra, Acadia, Osiris and Lilooet were taken to Shedd Aquarium in Chicago, while belugas Bertie Botts and Frankie were taken to SeaWorld in San Antonio, CBC reported.

“This was a herculean effort and one that will continue over the coming weeks,” SeaWorld San Antonio wrote in a Facebook post.

Bertie Botts and Frankie were relocated to SeaWorld San Antonio with the help of dedicated animal care professionals from Shedd Aquarium, Georgia Aquarium and Mystic Aquarium, officials said.

Marineland closed after the death of the zoo’s owners, John Holer and Marie Holer, in 2024, according to CBC.

3 The belugas from Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, traveled by truck and plane to reach their new abodes. via REUTERS

3 Aquatic medicine specialists, veterinarians and caretakers accompanied the majestic sea canaries on their international voyage. via REUTERS

Twenty whales had ended up dead at the park since 2019, according to data obtained by the Canadian Press.

Though staff continued to care for the sea creatures, the whales’ fate was in the balance for some time.

The park had said in October that it would be forced to euthanize the whales soon if a transfer wasn’t approved, the outlet reported.

Authorities in Canada eventually reached a deal with the US and Spain to move the whales to accredited facilities earlier last month.

topic: Darline Graham Lindsey Grahams Sister Full

left.title: Darline Graham, Lindsey Graham’s sister, to run for full Senate term

left-center.title: Lindsey Graham’s Sister, Darline Graham, Says She Will Run to Succeed Him

center.title: 'I'm in.' Darline Graham says she will run for a full term in Lindsey Graham's Senate seat

right-center.title: Nancy Mace reveals Senate decision as Lindsey Graham's sister redraws the race

right.title: Darline Graham Says She'll Run for Full 6-Year Term

farLeftText:
Darline Graham, the South Carolina interim senator and sister of the late Lindsey Graham, said she plans to run for a full Senate term after Donald Trump endorsed her last week.

In an interview with Sean Hannity that will air in full on Monday, Graham – who was appointed by Governor Henry McMaster to finish the remainder of her brother’s term – said she had “made a decision”.

“I’m in,” she told the Fox News anchor.

Graham, 62, made history as the first woman to represent South Carolina in the Senate when she was sworn in last week.

Her brother, Lindsey died earlier this month, aged 71, after what his office called a “brief and sudden illness”. The chief medical examiner preliminarily ruled that he died of aortic dissection due to cardiovascular disease.

On Friday, Trump said he had encouraged Darline Graham to run for a full six-year term representing South Carolina.

“I asked Darline, for the Good of our Nation, to run for the U.S. Senate in the Special Republican Primary,” he wrote on Truth Social. “Darline, who comes from an absolutely incredible family, has been a WINNER all of her life and, should she accept, has my Complete and Total Endorsement.”

Because Lindsey Graham won the Republican nomination last month, the state will now hold a special primary to replace him on the ballot on 11 August.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: "I've made a decision," Darline Graham told Fox News Channel host Sean Hannity , according to a clip released of an interview set to air later Monday. "I'm in." The entrance of Darline Graham — sworn in last week to fulfill the remaining months of her late brother's term, which expires in January — into the Republican scramble to select a new Senate nominee further complicates an already rushed process following Lindsey Graham 's death earlier this month. A weeklong filing period opens Tuesday for the special Republican primary, which will be Aug. 11. On Friday, President Donald Trump said that Darline Graham had his "Complete and Total Endorsement" to seek the nomination, adding, "RUN, DARLINE, RUN!" READ MORE: Who is Darline Graham, the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's sister? Last week, Gov. Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham to serve the remainder of her brother's term, which ends in January. In his announcement, McMaster made no reference to Darline Graham as a placeholder or symbolic appointment, although a person familiar with McMaster's thinking but unauthorized to speak publicly said the governor, in selecting Darline Graham, had never contemplated that she would run for the seat herself. Before the emergence of Darline Graham — and their Oval Office meeting after she was sworn in — Trump had previously suggested he could back a potential candidacy from Rep. Russell Fry, who said Monday he would officially enter the special primary. Several other noteworthy politicians, including Rep. Ralph Norman, have already gotten in, and others like Rep. Nancy Mace and Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette have been eyeing a run. Watch the clip in the player above. Darline Graham told Hannity said she had been "praying a lot" about how to navigate her new role. "It is a tremendous amount of pressure," she said. "I know I'm a hard worker. I learned that from Lindsey," Darline Graham said. "I feel like I can do it. I feel an inner peace about it. Will it be difficult? Yes." READ MORE: Darline Graham's career centered disability. Now she could help shape national policy Funeral services for Lindsey Graham are scheduled to be held next week in Washington and South Carolina. A preliminary report from the medical examiner said he suffered a tear in his aorta. The millions in Lindsey Graham's campaign account aren't funds that Darline Graham could directly access in a run of her own, according to Bradley A. Smith, a former chairman of the Federal Election Commission. Under federal rules, Lindsey Graham's campaign would be limited to transferring just $2,000 to a potential Darline Graham candidacy. However, Smith said there is no limit on how much it could transfer to the National Republican Senatorial Committee, which could — thanks to a Supreme Court decision last month — "spend an unlimited amount in coordination with Darline's campaign."
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! As the field for the race to replace the late Sen. Lindsey Graham takes shape, one prominent lawmaker is declining to dive into the arena hours after the contest was shaken up. Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., opted against a bid after she strongly suggested that she’d throw her hat into the arena on Monday in a video statement. Last August, Mace launched a campaign for the Republican gubernatorial nomination in the race to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Henry McMaster, but she finished a disappointing fifth out of five major candidates in last month’s Republican primary. "I want to be straight with you, I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now," Mace said. "I'm not chasing another title, I'm chasing justice, justice for me, justice for other women and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent," she continued. "That's where my focus is today, and that's where it's going to stay." FOX NEWS CHANNEL’S 'HANNITY' TO AIR EXCLUSIVE FIRST INTERVIEW WITH SEN DARLINE GRAHAM ON MONDAY Her decision followed a move by Sen. Darline Graham, R-S.C., to take up her brother's mantle after she was appointed to finish off the remainder of her late brother's term. She made her decision to officially enter the upcoming special election after a push from President Donald Trump. Graham, during an interview with Fox News’ Sean Hannity, acknowledged the "tremendous amount of pressure" to take over the position and run. "I feel like I can do it, I feel an inner peace about it," Graham said. "Will it be difficult? Yes." When asked if she had made a decision on what she would do, she told Hannity she had. "I'm in," Graham said. Her addition to the growing roster of hopefuls to replace the late lawmaker will likely shake up the race less than one day before candidates can begin officially filing to run for the nomination. Graham will come up against other challengers in her bid to claim the GOP nomination. Reps. Ralph Norman, R-S.C., Russell Fry, R-S.C., and businessman Mark Lynch, have all publicly committed to running. And as the filing deadline fast approaches, both Mace and Norman turned their steel against their colleague, Fry, in what’s shaping up to be a messy, Palmetto State brawl. Mace argued that should Fry win the nomination, and subsequently the general election, he would vacate his "Republican House seat until next May." REPUBLICANS DEFY TRUMP, JUMP INTO SOUTH CAROLINA SENATE RACE DESPITE HIS HANDPICKED SUCCESSOR "That narrows an already razor-thin majority and puts the Speaker's gavel in play," Mace said. "I will never hand Hakeem Jeffries that gift." "If you're running for two offices at once, you've already told us who you are," she continued. "That's not about politics anymore — that's about power. You're either with the people you serve or you're not." Fry announced Monday morning that he would enter the race, hours after Norman tossed in his hat, too. Mace has been making public overtures that she wants to jump in, but has so far not made anything official. A source in Fry’s political orbit told Fox News Digital that the lawmaker has "accomplished more than all the potential candidates combined," having passed three bills in his first four years in Congress. "This is really about giving voters a choice and not a coronation," they said. Still, the conservative duo are going for blood against Fry, arguing that his bid for the seat is purely politically motivated and jeopardizes the House’s already slim majority heading into the midterm elections later this year. Under South Carolina law, Fry can appear for multiple offices on the ballot. He was unopposed for his primary election for South Carolina’s 7th congressional district, and if he wins the nomination, he’ll be on the ballot twice for both. TRUMP REVEALS WHO HE'S EYEING TO REPLACE LINDSEY GRAHAM Ultimately, he’ll have to choose if he wins both, which would trigger another special election to fill his place. The top two Republicans in the South Carolina Statehouse, South Carolina Senate President Thomas Alexander and South Carolina House Speaker Murrell Smith, said in a statement that the upcoming special election has "highlighted issues in South Carolina law," and noted that, "In the coming weeks, we intend to address these important issues so that we can provide the process available for our citizens." That is not a problem for either Mace nor Norman, who both ran and failed to clinch the Republican gubernatorial nomination last month. "SC Republican voters soundly rejected Nancy and Ralph as recently as last month because conservatives are tired of career politicians who care more about their own social media than getting actual results for families," South Carolina state Sen. JD Chaplin, and a spokesperson for Fry's campaign, told Fox News Digital. Norman was less venomous in his take on the situation, and noted on X that he respected Fry and had enjoyed his time serving with him in the House. Still, his candidacy, he argued, "could leave his seat vacant until May 18th." "This would leave his constituents unrepresented, jeopardize President Trump’s agenda, and put the House at risk of being led by a Speaker Hakeem Jeffries," Norman said. "We can’t let that happen!!" CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Senate Majority Leader Sen. John Thune, speaking to reporters on Monday afternoon, referred to Darlene Graham and said, "She obviously carries on Lindsey's legacy. But I think she's a very accomplished person in her own right." Asked if he would be endorsing her, the longtime lawmaker from South Dakota and top Republican in the Senate said, "I'm not endorsing anyone at the moment." Fox News' Tyler Olson contributed to this report

topic: Sentenced Life Drug Mayo Prison Cartel

left.title: Mexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life in prison in US federal court

center.title: Notorious drug cartel leader Ismael Zambada sentenced to life in US prison

right-center.title: Sinaloa Cartel boss 'El Mayo' sentenced to life, ordered to pay $15B in drug trafficking profits

right.title: Sinaloa Cartel Kingpin 'El Mayo' Sentenced in U.S. to Life in Prison

farLeftText: The Mexican drug kingpin, Ismael Zambada García, known as “El Mayo”, was sentenced in a US federal court on Monday to life in prison, after a plea deal last year in which he admitted ⁠to shipping vast amounts of cocaine and other substances as leader of the violent Sinaloa cartel. Zambada is 76 and one of the most high-profile Mexican ⁠drug traffickers to be sentenced in a US court since his fellow Sinaloa leader Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán ​was sentenced to life in prison in ‌2019. The case unfolded in federal court in Brooklyn, New York. Zambada helped co-found and lead the cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades. He signed a plea deal last August, admitting charges including racketeering conspiracy and continuing criminal enterprise, after the ‌US justice department said it would not seek the death penalty. The court also ordered him to forfeit $15bn from drug trade profits as part of his punishment. As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. He was accused of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world. Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents. Zambada, who has spent nearly two years inside a Brooklyn federal prison, limped inside the courtroom. He was wearing a beige prison outfit and had white hair and a beard. Before his sentencing, Zambada read a public letter, saying that he took responsibility for his crimes and calling for an end to the violence that has plagued Mexico for decades during the drug-fueled conflict. “I did not go to trial because I did not want to deny the truth,” Zambada read aloud in court, adding that “violence must end” and urging the next generation of drug traffickers to “choose a different path”. While El Chapo is better known as the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly El Mayo was regarded as the elusive, behind the scenes leader running the operations, with a low profile and few public sightings. El Mayo’s arrest in the US in 2024 came as a surprise. During a mysterious operation, he was known to have been beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons, Joaquín Guzmán López, and was flown across the US border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents. It remains unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. Guzmán López pled guilty in an abduction case late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US justice department. El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions, in a raging intra-cartel battle for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico. El Mayo’s sentencing comes at a tense time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to crack down harder on criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened US military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug-trafficking organizations. US district judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial, has been hearing the case in New York. Cogan acknowledged that the sentencing hearing was “anticlimactic” because of the plea agreement. All parties involved understood that Zambada would spend the rest of his life in prison, the judge said. Frank Perez, a lawyer for El Mayo, ​had said in a 6 July court filing that his client was fully aware he would receive a life sentence when he decided to accept responsibility for his crimes. Perez asked Cogan to recommend to the US Bureau of Prisons that his client, as a septuagenarian, be placed in a ⁠facility where he would receive adequate medical care. Prosecutors with the Brooklyn US attorney’s office said the prisons ​suggested by Perez may not ​be equipped to handle the security concerns posed by Zambada, including the ​possibility that he could ‌continue to direct ​cartel activities from ​behind bars. “Those security concerns will continue for the foreseeable future because individuals loyal to the defendant continue to control the cartel,” the prosecutors wrote in a 13 July filing. Zambada’s defense attorneys on Monday requested that the judge recommend a federal medical facility, rather than a traditional prison, due to his medical conditions. Health details are redacted in court records, but the judge mentioned during the sentencing hearing that Zambada is “drifting” towards being “cognitively impaired”. Mexican outlets have reported Zambada is dealing with the early stages of dementia. The Bureau of Prisons still has to evaluate where Zambada will be sent to serve the rest of his days. El Chapo is serving his life sentence at a maximum-security prison in Colorado. Frank Perez, one of Zambada’s attorneys, said during the sentencing hearing that his client did not cooperate with the US government, neither providing information to US officials about other cartel members or leaders, nor Mexican officials who have protected the cartel’s drug-trafficking business. Reuters contributed reporting
